coredata.dc:title: Efficient core shell structured dual response ratiometric fluorescence probe for determination of H2O2 and glucose via etching of silver nanoprisms
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Hongzhi
Yu, Chunwei
Zhang, Yue
Xu, Shoufang
coredata.dc:description: A simple and sensitive inner filter effect and charge transfer dual response ratiometric fluorescent probe (D-RFP) for sensing glucose was developed based on etching of silver nanoprisms (Ag NPRs). The D-RFP was proposed by hybridizing red emitting CdTe QDs and blue emitting CDs into core-shell structured silica nanoparticles. In this design, when mixed Ag NPRs with the D-RFP, QDs which embedded in silica nanoparticles can be quenched by Ag NPRs via inner filter effect. Ag NPRs can be effectively etched to silver ions by H2O2 which can bind with CDs and quench CDs by charge transfer. The etching of Ag NPRs leaded to the recovering the fluorescence of QDs and quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Under optimal condition, D-RFP can be exploited for H2O2 detection with detection limit of 0.28 μM and glucose sensing with detection limit of 0.64 μM. The developed approach was applied to monitor glucose levels in human serum samples with satisfactory results. Moreover, this method provided a new idea for designing D-RFP and can be extended to other detection systems based on silver nanoparticles (including Ag NPs, Ag NPRs, or Au@Ag nanorods) etching process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Serum glucose
Silver nanoprisms etching
Dual response mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdTe QDs, Ag NPRs and CDs
Detection of glucose based on D-RFP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21777065 ) and Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China ( ZR2018BEE017 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of blue emissive CDs, red emissive QDs and the D-RFPs. The inset photos show the corresponding fluorescence colors under a 365 nm UV lamp, respectively; (B) The UV–vis spectrum of Ag NPRs with the addition of different concentration of H2O2. Inset photos and TEM images were the Ag NPRs with different concentration of H2O2. From left to right was Ag NPRs, Ag NPRs+ 100 μM of H2O2, Ag NPRs+ 1 mM of H2O2. (C) Fluorescence spectra of D-RFPs with 0, 100 and 200 μM of H2O2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescence titrations of the D-RFPs (100 μg mL−1) with Ag NPRs from 5 to 40 μM; (B) Quenching efficiency of D-RFP (100 μg mL−1) versus the concentration Ag NPRs at 625 nm; (C) Fluorescence response of D-RFP and Ag NPRs (100 μg mL−1 and 20 μM, respectively) in the presence of different concentration of H2O2; (D) The relationship between ratio of fluorescence intensity of the D-RFP in (C) versus concentration of H2O2.

The corresponding fluorescence spectra (A) and changes of fluorescence intensity (B) of D-RFPs upon the exposure to different concentrations of glucose. The corresponding fluorescence spectra and changes of fluorescence intensity of reference RFPs (C) and single emission fluorescence probe (D) upon the exposure to different concentrations of glucose.

(A) The color change of the three different fluorescence probes with different amount of glucose under 365 nm UV light (a D-RFPs; b reference RFPs and c was single emission fluorescence probes). From left to right, 0.5. 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 125, 150 μM; d was Color responses of D-RFPs to 3 healthy persons and 3 diabetes patients. (B) Selectivity of the assay. Experimental condition: ratiometric probe: 100 μg mL−1, AgNPRs: 20 μM, GOx: 30 mg/mL. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of D-RFP and detection principle for H2O2 and glucose.

head.ce:title: Efficient core shell structured dual response ratiometric fluorescence probe for determination of H2O2 and glucose via etching of silver nanoprisms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi, 276005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Environmental Monitoring, School of Tropical and Laboratory Medicine, Hainan Medical University, Haikou, 571101, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Tropical and Laboratory Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi, 276005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and sensitive inner filter effect and charge transfer dual response ratiometric fluorescent probe (D-RFP) for sensing glucose was developed based on etching of silver nanoprisms (Ag NPRs). The D-RFP was proposed by hybridizing red emitting CdTe QDs and blue emitting CDs into core-shell structured silica nanoparticles. In this design, when mixed Ag NPRs with the D-RFP, QDs which embedded in silica nanoparticles can be quenched by Ag NPRs via inner filter effect. Ag NPRs can be effectively etched to silver ions by H2O2 which can bind with CDs and quench CDs by charge transfer. The etching of Ag NPRs leaded to the recovering the fluorescence of QDs and quenching the fluorescence of CDs. Under optimal condition, D-RFP can be exploited for H2O2 detection with detection limit of 0.28 μM and glucose sensing with detection limit of 0.64 μM. The developed approach was applied to monitor glucose levels in human serum samples with satisfactory results. Moreover, this method provided a new idea for designing D-RFP and can be extended to other detection systems based on silver nanoparticles (including Ag NPs, Ag NPRs, or Au@Ag nanorods) etching process.

Dual response ratiometric fluorescent assay for sensing glucose was proposed based on inner filter effect and charge transfer coupled with the etching of Ag nanoprisms.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric fluorescence probes (RFPs) are a class of special fluorescent probes which displayed two independent fluorescent emission peaks under single excitation wavelength. Due to its advantages in terms of higher selectivity and resisting environmental disturbances, RFPs has been widely concerned and developed rapidly in recent years. In addition, changes in the ratio of fluorescence (FL) intensity will bring about a significant fluorescence color change, and visualization of the analyte can be achieved using RFPs. Up to date, many fluorophores, such as fluorescent organic dyes [1], quantum dots [2], carbon dots [3], nobel metal nanoclusters [4], had been employed to construct RFP. Most RFPs belonged to reference RFPs, in which the FL intensity of one fluorophores changed while the other keep constant as a reference. Many ways can be used to construct reference RFPs, such as core-shell structure [5,6] or chemical coupling [7] or linked directly by the target [8]. Due to the relatively simple construction process, core-shell structured RFPs were the most common employed method. Generally, the reference fluorophores were embedded into silica particles, polymers or nanogels while the response fluorophores were introduce onto the surface of the silica by physical absorption or chemical conjugation. In generally, dual response RFPs (the FL intensity of both the two fluorophores in the RFPs changed with the addition of the target [9,10]. Usually one increases and the other decreases) displayed higher sensitivity and more obvious color change. However, most of the dual response RFPs were designed based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer and always involved in a complex chemical synthesis process. It was imperative to develop a simple and efficient dual response RFPs.

Among those response modes for fluorescence sensors, inner filter effect (IFE) was flexible and simple compared to electron transfer or resonance energy transfer. There was no necessary to consider the distance between fluorophores or functional groups of those fluorophores. The overlap between the absorption spectra of the absorbers and the fluorescence spectra of fluorophores was the foundation of IFE. Nobel metal nanoparticles, such as gold nanoparticles [11,12], silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) [13–15], with unique optical characteristics, gained special interest in the development of IFE based optical sensors. Based on the fact that Ag NPs could be etched by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to silver ions (Ag+), and Ag NPs can quench fluorophores by IFE, many sensing platforms had been constructed [13–15]. However, all the above-mentioned works were single emission fluorescence, not RFPs. In the meanwhile, Ag NPs were employed in the etching process and very few documents have reported that silver nanoprisms (Ag NPRs) [16,17] were etched.

It is well known that higher blood sugar levels can lead to a variety of serious diseases. Therefore, effective determination of the blood glucose concentration is particularly important. In recent years, a variety of sensing technologies have been developed for glucose detection. For example, fluorescence [15], colorimetry [18] based gold nanoparticle, electrochemistry [19] and Raman scattering [20]. The color of the blood itself would interferes with the observation of color change in the colorimetric detection. Electrodes employed in electrochemical sensing are susceptible to contamination by proteins. The instrument required to detect Raman spectroscopy is expensive. Single emission fluorescent probes could be influenced by fluctuation of excitation light intensity, and environmental interference. Therefore, it was necessary to develop a detection method with advantages of on-site, fast, accurate, sensitivity and low price.

Inspired by the above-mentioned works, we attempted to design a sensitive core shell structured dual response RFP (D-RFP) for detection of glucose based on the following facts. Firstly, glucose can be oxidase to generate H2O2. Secondly, Ag NPRs can be etched to silver ions by H2O2 [21]. Thirdly, QDs with maximum emission spectrum at 625 nm can be quenched by Ag NPRs with maximum absorption peak at 625 nm by IFE. Finally, CDs can be quenched by silver ions by charge transfer [22]. In our designed core shell structure nanoparticle hybrids, QDs were encapsulated into silica particles while CDs were grafted onto the surface of the silica. Ag NPRs quenched the FL intensity of QDs based on IFE. Ag NPRs can be effectively etched to silver ions by H2O2 which was generated by glucose-glucose oxidase enzymatic reaction, leading to the recovery of QDs and decrease of CDs. The innovation of this article was reflected in the following aspects. First, unlike previous reported core-shell structured RFP (fluorophore coated in the silica only acted as reference), QDs coated inside the silica sphere also acted as response signal by IFE. Second, unlike previous reported optical sensor based on Ag NPs etching, Ag NPRs etching was employed in this work and etched products (Ag+) also participate in the detection response. Third and most important, dual response modes (IFE and charge transfer) were employed in this work. This dual response modes strategy proposed here greatly increase the detection sensitivity, and provided a new idea for designing high-sensitivity D-RFP.

1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were received from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), tellurium powder, silver nitrate, cadmium chloride hydrate, ammonium hydroxide, urea, ethanol, sodium citrate dehydrate, glycine, and were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose oxidase (GOx), glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose and sucrose were all obtained from Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Services Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). A Millipore water purification system was employed to provide Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm).

Fluorescence spectrum were performed on Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by UV-3600 ultraviolet spectrophotometry (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) were used for morphological evaluation. An AGL-9406 portable UV lamp was used for taking fluorescence photos. A FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet Corporation, USA) was employed to examine the infrared spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated using ESCALAB 250 spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdTe QDs, Ag NPRs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs were prepared as our previous work [23]. CDs were prepared through microwave method using glycine and urea as resources [22]. Ag NPRs were synthesized using the following method [17]. Briefly, AgNO3 (2.0 mM, 2.5 mL), sodium citrate solution (30 mM, 3 mL), PVP (0.7 mM, 3 mL) and H2O2 (30 wt%, 120 μL) were added to a flask containing 45 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the quick addition of fresh prepared NaBH4 (100 mM, 180 μL). The reaction was carried out in room temperature for 30 min to achieve a blue solution.

The core shell structured D-RFP was proposed by two steps. For the first step, QDs were embedded into silica nanoparticles by typical reverse micro-emulsion method followed by the modification of silica surface with amino groups [24]. Then carboxylic groups modified CDs were chemically bonded to the amino-modified silica nanoparticles by a condensation reaction with the help of EDC/NHS [7]. The resultant D-RFP was dispersed in ultrapure water for further use.

For detection of glucose in buffers, various concentrations of glucose was incubated with GOx (30 μg mL−1) at 37 °C for 1.5 h to generate H2O2. Then the mixture of Ag NPRs (20 μM), D-RFP (100 mg/L) and phosphate buffer was added to the above reaction solution, followed by the record of FL spectrum of D-RFP. For the selectivity of the assay, a number of related carbohydrates, including fructose, maltose, lactose and sucrose were tested under the same assay procedures described above except different carbohydrates were used.

The human serum samples used in this experiment were provide by a local hospital. Prior to analysis, human serum albumin was separated and removed from the samples by centrifuge. Then diluted serum sample by a factor of 200 and GOx (30 μg mL−1) in phosphate buffer was incubated at 37 °C for 1.5 h. Then the mixture of Ag NPRs, D-RFP and phosphate buffer was added to the above reaction solution, followed by the record of FL spectrum of D-RFP. These clinical blood samples were also analyzed using a commercial automatic biochemical analyzer for control. In the meanwhile, visual detection was performed under a UV lamp.

The synthesis procedure of the core shell structured D-RFP was shown in Scheme 1 . First, CdTe QDs were fully wrapped into silica nanoparticles by reverse micro-emulsion method, followed by the surface modification of silica particles with amino groups. The amino groups on the silica surface was confirmed by FT-IR (Fig. S1A). Reverse micro-emulsion method was adopted to coat QDs because silica prepared by this method had a regular spherical morphology and a larger specific surface area, which was facilitate for the linkage of CDs. CDs prepared by microwave method using glycine and urea as resource were characterized detailed by FT-IR and XPS (Fig. S1B). FT-IR spectrum and XPS date confirmed the existence of carboxylic groups on the surface of CDs. Then CDs were chemically bonded to the silica nanoparticles by a condensation reaction between amino groups and carboxylic groups with the catalysis of EDC and NHS. TEM images and fluorescence spectrum confirmed the successful preparation of the material. TEM images (Fig. S2) indicated QDs@SiO2 with smooth surface displayed regular sphere morphology, and the average size was about 70 nm. After anchoring CDs, the diameter of the D-RFP (QDs@SiO2@CDs) did not show obvious increase, but the surfaces became relatively rough. The fluorescence spectrum also verified the successful preparation of the D-RFP (Fig. 1 A). QDs@SiO2 exhibited a strong red fluorescence under a UV lamp (λ = 365 nm). After anchoring blue CDs to the surface of silica nanoparticles, the corresponding fluorescence color of the D-RFP was pink, which was different from the blue CDs and red QDs. Furthermore, the D-RFPs displayed well-resolved dual emission bands (λ = 625 nm and λ = 400 nm) under a single wavelength excitation at 345 nm. Reference reported that QDs also can be etched by H2O2 [25], so CdTe QDs were embedded into silica in our work. With the protection of silica shell, the etching and quenching of CdTe QDs can be avoided. The signal output and sensitivity can be improved by the core shell structure.

The detection mechanism was also displayed in Scheme 1. When mixed Ag NPRs with the D-RFP, the fluorescence of the QDs would significantly quenched due to the inner filtering effect between Ag NPRs and QDs. When further adding H2O2 to the system, Ag NPRs were etched to silver ions, which bind with CDs and quenched CDs by charge transfer, which resulted in the recovering of FL of QDs and decrease of FL of CDs. Considering that glucose can be conversed to H2O2 with the catalysis of GOx, the strategy can be employed for specifically monitoring glucose. With changes of the ratio FL intensity of CDs and QDs, the D-RFPs showed a significant change in fluorescence color from blue to pink to red.

The detection mechanism and feasibility of this method were confirmed in detail. First, the role of Ag NPRs was verified. Ag NPRs served a dual function of a quencher and a H2O2 recognizer. When added Ag NPRs to QDs solution, the FL of QDs decreased sharply (Fig. S3A). The quenching mechanism between QDs and Ag NPRs was based on inner filter effect, which was confirmed from the overlapped emission spectrum of QDs and absorption spectrum of Ag NPRs (Fig. S3B). The decomposition of Ag NPRs by H2O2 etching was confirmed by UV–vis absorption spectra and TEM images. As depicted in Fig. 1B, the characteristic absorbance of Ag NPRs at 625 nm obviously decreases with the increase of H2O2. In the meanwhile, the color of Ag NPRs solution changed from blue to purple to colorless, and the morphology changed from Ag NPRs to silver disks, indicating the Ag NPRs were destroyed by H2O2. As the Ag NPRs were etched, the FL of the QDs was restored, as seen from Fig. S3A. During etching process, the Ag NPRs were oxidized to silver ions, which can bind with CDs and depress the FL of CDs, as shown in Fig. S3C. The mechanism of CDs quenched by silver ions was based on charge transfer, as reported by previous work [22].

The possibility of glucose sensing was explored by mixing the D-RFPs, Ag NPRs and H2O2 by sequence. As expected, the addition of Ag NPRs resulted in the decrease of FL intensity of QDs at 625 nm. Then a significant FL recovery at 625 nm and decrease at 400 nm for this D-RFPs was observed upon the introduction of H2O2 (Fig. 1C). The result confirmed the feasibility of our fabricated D-RFPs for H2O2 detection. At the same time, this method can be used to detect glucose.

The photo stability and chemical stability were critical for their meaningful applications, which were systematically investigated. Fig. S4indicated that the FL intensity of CDs and the relative FL intensity of D-RFPs had no apparent change when undergo continuous excitation at 365 nm for 60 min or under different concentrations of sodium salts. The results suggest that the as-prepared D-RFPs showed good photo stability and chemical stability.

To acquire ideal performance for detection of H2O2, the concentration of D-RFP and the amount of Ag NPRs were optimized. The concentrations of D-RFP and Ag NPRs mainly effect the detection sensitivity and background signal. Considering that the amount of Ag NPRs was directly affected by the amount of D-RFP, the concentration of RFP and Ag NPRs were examined together. The usage concentrations of D-RFP (e.g., 50, 100 and 150 μg mL−1) (Fig. S5, Fig. S6, Fig. 2 ) were investigated. It should be pointed out that the concentrations of Ag NPRs herein were always chosen at the inflection points for the subsequent detection of H2O2, which allow one to obtain maximum detection range and sensitivity [26]. Table S1 summarized the usage of Ag NPRs, the linear rang and LOD of detection H2O2 under different D-RFPs dosages. Take Fig. 2 as an example. The FL intensity of the two well-resolved emission peaks of the D-RFPs was strong. When mixed D-RFPs (fixed as 100 mg/L) with different amount of Ag NPRs, the FL intensity of QD gradually decreases when increasing the dosage of Ag NPRs from 5 to 40 μM. When the amount of Ag NPRs is 20 μM, an inflection point appears on Fig. 2B. When mixed D-RFPs (fixed as 100 mg/L) with Ag NPRs (fixed as 20 μM), FL intensity of CDs was higher than QDs due to the quench of Ag NPRs. Upon the addition of H2O2, the FL intensity of QDs continuously recovered while the FL intensity of CDs decreased. The ratio of the FL intensity (I625/I400) displayed good linearity to the amount of H2O2 over the range of 1–120 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.996. The detection limit was 0.28 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Through comprehensive consideration of both sensitivity and range of linear detection, 100 μg mL−1 of D-RFP and 20 μM of Ag NPRs were selected as the optimized conditions for detection of H2O2 and glucose, respectively.

The advantage of ratio fluorescence detection was reflected when compared with the colorimetric sensors based on etching of Ag NPRs. Fig. S7 displayed the UV–vis spectrum of Ag NPRs etched by different concentration of H2O2, the linear range and the color change of Ag NPRs under optimum condition. The wavelength shift of Ag NPRs was closely related to the amount of H2O2, ranging from 80 to 220 μM with the detection limit of 32.4 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose based on D-RFP
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of D-RFPs for H2O2 generated reaction was verified by glucose sensing. Incubation glucose, GOx with D-RFPs/Ag NPRs hybrid probes resulted in an obvious ratiometric response output. In contrast, sole addition of glucose or GOx did not induce obvious signal change. In order to get the best sensing performance, the amount of GOx and incubation time were optimized. The experimental results indicated that 30 mg/mL GOx was sufficient for 300 μM glucose oxidation, and 90 min was enough for the catalysis reaction time. Then glucose detection was performed under the above optimal conditions (Fig. 3 A). The FL intensity ratio between 625 and 400 nm (I625/I400) was increased with increasing glucose concentration, varying from 0 to 150 μM. I625/I400 and the concentration of glucose present a good linear relationship in the range of 2–90 μM and 90–150 μM, respectively. The detection limit of glucose was 0.64 μM based on the equation of 3σ/S (Fig. 3B). Moreover, the advantages of the D-RFP was reflected by comparison with the reference RFPs in which only IFE was employed for the detection of H2O2 (QDs and CDs were both embedding into silica nanoparticles, CDs were used as reference while QDs as used as response signal by IFE) (Fig. 3C) and single-emission fluorescent probe (just embedding QDs into silica nanoparticles) (Fig. 3D). The linear range and LOD was summarized in Table S2. By contrast Fig. 3, we found that the sensitivity of D-RFP was higher than the reference RFPs, while single-emission fluorescent probe displayed the lowest sensitivity. The comparison clearly shows that the D-RFP possesses highest sensitivity and reliability.

D-RFPs was employed for visual semiquantification detection of glucose (Fig. 4 A). After addition of different concentrations of glucose to D-RFPs/Ag NPRs hybrid probes, the fluorescence colors of the solution changed continuously from blue to pink, then to red, owing to the changes in the FL intensity ratio. In the meanwhile, the color change of single-emission fluorescent probes and single-response RFP were not obvious as the D-RFPs. The results (Table S3) indicated that this D-RFPs was more sensitive in visual detection. Furthermore, when compared the sensitivity of this work with some excellent published works for glucose detection, D-RFP displayed comparable or even higher sensitive.

The selectivity of the as prepared D-RFPs toward glucose was tested using relevant glucides (fructose, sucrosem, altose, and lactose) as control. As expected, only glucose induced an obvious signal output while other interfering substances could not cause significant interference (Fig. 4B). The assay thus shows excellent selectivity for glucose. This superior selectivity may attributed to the specific selectivity of the GOx enzyme.

The feasibility of D-RFPs for detecting glucose in real samples was further investigated in human serum samples. Considering that human serum albumin (HSA) in serum would influence the fluorescence detection, samples were centrifuged to remove HSA [25]. Taking into consideration the normal glucose level in healthy human blood (3.9–6.1 mM [25]) as well as the linear range of our method, the human serum samples were diluted by 200 times to make the concentration of glucose be in the linear range of this proposed method. The developed method was applied to detect six individual real samples including three healthy volunteers and three diabetes volunteers. Standard addition method was employed for detection glucose concentration in real samples using D-RFPs. The determined glucose concentrations using this method and a commercial automatic biochemical analyzer were listed in Table S4. The results clearly demonstrate the feasibility of this method for accurate glucose detection in human serum samples. In addition, spike experiments were also adopted and results were listed in Table S4. The recoveries ranged from 98.5% to 101.2% for the six real samples, and a relative standard deviation (RSD) no more than 6% was obtained. To achieve visualize detection of glucose in serum samples, the real samples were observed under a UV lamp with 365 nm. From Fig. 4A we can see that the fluorescent color of the three samples from the healthy volunteer were pink while the samples from the volunteer with diabetes were red. Compared with the single-emission probe, this dual emission was more easy to distinguish the healthy and diabetes by the naked eye under a UV lamp.

In summary, we have developed a novel D-RFP for detection of glucose by QDs@CDs/Ag NPRs hybrid probe based on inner filter effect and charger transfer dual response mechanism. The fluorescence of QDs wsa quenched due to IFE between QDs and Ag NPRs. Ag NPRs are etched to Ag+ by H2O2 which was generated by enzymatic oxidation of glucose, leading to a recovery of QDs and decrease of CDs, and the ratio of FL intensity (I625/I400) can response to the glucose concentration with good linear relationship. This method was highlighted by the ingenious design and its excellent sensitivity toward glucose. The accurate detection of glucose in complex human serum samples confirmed the potential practical application of the probe. The present concept not only provides a method for the sensing of H2O2-related reactions but also represents a promising designation of D-RFPs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21777065) and Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (ZR2018BEE017).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.10.025.
